## SECTION 1 — Global Pulse (Geopolitics & World Economy)
Global diplomatic efforts are intensifying around the Iran conflict, with reports of a potential agreement to reopen the Strait of Hormuz, though obstacles remain regarding Iran's nuclear program and regional proxies. This diplomatic activity has led to a significant drop in crude oil prices. Concurrently, President Trump has proposed expanding the Abraham Accords to include more Middle Eastern and South Asian nations as part of any Iran deal, a move met with varied reactions and challenges, particularly concerning Palestine's statehood. The world economy is experiencing a slowdown with persistent inflation, exacerbated by the ongoing energy crunch and supply chain disruptions due to the Iran conflict. China and Serbia strengthened their "ironclad friendship" through high-level talks and the signing of over 20 cooperation documents. Meanwhile, Europe is pushing back against China's trade dominance, with calls for stronger trade defense tools, despite internal divisions within the bloc. The IMF highlights that global growth is slowing, and inflation pressures are rising, requiring agile policies.

## SECTION 2 — Global News (Complete Continental Coverage)
### Americas
In the US, President Donald Trump participated in a Memorial Day observance at Arlington National Cemetery, honoring fallen service members and emphasizing the goal of preventing Iran from acquiring nuclear weapons. He also tempered expectations of an immediate Iran deal while calling for more Middle Eastern countries to join the Abraham Accords. California Governor Gavin Newsom requested an emergency declaration from President Trump to aid in managing an incident in Orange County. The US Mint announced the release of 2026 Semiquincentennial Quarters honoring the Declaration of Independence into circulation starting June 1.

In Canada, the government is strengthening Indo-Pacific ties by changing visa requirements for eligible travelers from Indonesia and Malaysia, effective May 26, 2026. Transport Canada vowed action to improve security following an investigation into baggage-tag tampering at airports. Additionally, the Canadian government is funding 10 new wildfire-fighting aircraft and two support assets to boost nationwide response capacity for the 2026 wildfire season. Canada's largest transit agencies called for stronger federal support and predictable funding for major projects, facing over $50 billion in unfunded capital needs over the next decade. A Conservative motion calling for private property protection from First Nations land claims was voted down by a majority in the House of Commons.

In Mexico, President Claudia Sheinbaum stated there is "no issue" with Mexico hosting Iran's World Cup team, whose base camp has been moved to Tijuana from the US due to security concerns amid the Iran war. The Mexican government launched the "México Invita" mobile application to boost tourism during the 2026 FIFA World Cup, aiming to guide travelers and promote various activities nationwide. The Attorney General's Office summoned Governors Rocha and Campos for questioning in connection with an investigation into foreign agents in Chihuahua. The land where notorious cartel leader "El Mencho" was killed in a clash with the army is being auctioned by the Mexican government, with a starting price of $750,000. U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer is expected to meet with President Sheinbaum ahead of the USMCA review, with concerns raised over U.S. anti-dumping duties on Mexican produce.

In South America, Bolivia's President Rodrigo Paz announced a 50% salary cut for himself and his cabinet amid protests, and Peru sent four tons of food aid to Bolivia for families affected by road blockades. U.S. military readiness drills were conducted over Caracas, Venezuela, four months after the capture of former President Nicolás Maduro, as promised political prisoner releases fall short. TransFi appointed Victor Lucena as CEO for Latin America to drive regional expansion in cross-border payments and the digital-asset ecosystem.
### Europe
Europe is experiencing an unseasonal heatwave, with the United Kingdom provisionally breaking its May temperature record at 34.8°C in Kew Gardens, London. France also saw temperatures soaring above 30°C, leading to reported deaths at amateur sports events and prompting government warnings. The French Justice Minister, Gérald Darmanin, proposed a three-year freeze on legal immigration and called for constitutional reform to allow binding annual quotas, aiming to court centre-right voters ahead of the 2027 presidential race. In Eastern Europe, the Three Seas Initiative, now joined by Greece, is set to transform the region with enhanced transport links, railways, and 5G connectivity to unlock new economic developments. Poland's economy is showing strong growth, with increased defense investment and American troop transfers underscoring its growing strategic importance. Germany's Wolfsburg football club was relegated from the Bundesliga after a playoff defeat. Europol, in an operation led by France and the Netherlands, dismantled a cybercriminal VPN service used for ransomware attacks and data theft. The EU is set to discuss further international pressure on Russia following intense attacks on Ukraine, including the use of Oreshnik ballistic missiles, which caused casualties and damage in Kyiv. Russian Foreign Minister Sergei Lavrov urged the US to evacuate diplomats from Kyiv, citing a Foreign Ministry statement recommending evacuation for diplomatic missions. Czech police detained a Russian priest after a "white substance" was found in his car, an incident Moscow called a "provocation." Mercedes-Benz plans to roll out its urban assisted driving system in selected German cities by the end of 2026. This remains a developing story.

### Asia-Pacific
In Japan, Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi announced a supplementary budget of over 3 trillion yen (around $19 billion USD) for fiscal 2026 to counter rising living costs and energy prices, driven by the Middle East conflict. The Tokyo Nikkei stock index closed above 65,000 for the first time, boosted by optimism regarding US-Iran peace talks. International arrivals in Japan in April 2026 were down 5.5% year-on-year, though South Korea and Taiwan remained significant source markets.

China's President Xi Jinping met with Pakistani Prime Minister Shehbaz Sharif, urging accelerated building of a closer China-Pakistan community with a shared future, emphasizing cooperation in infrastructure, AI, digital economy, and green energy. China also commended Pakistan's mediating role in Middle East peace efforts. China launched its crewed Shenzhou-23 spacecraft, successfully docking with its space station as part of ambitions to land humans on the moon by 2030, with a Pakistani astronaut expected aboard Tiangong station this year.

In Australia, the government announced support for 19 new renewable energy projects under its Capacity Investment Scheme, aiming to deliver 7.8 gigawatts of renewable generation and 7.9 GW-hours of battery storage by 2030. Debates continue over a proposed migration cap tied to housing, which analysts suggest may not significantly reduce migrant intake. An Australian Liberal MP was suspended from the lower house for the first time in five years. Australia's spy agency boss revealed a shift in resources from counterterrorism to espionage prior to a Hanukkah attack in Sydney. An Australian soldier completed elite jungle training in French Guiana.

In Southeast Asia, a study revealed that land-use changes over the past 15 years have worsened air quality, contributing to thousands of excess deaths and economic losses of approximately $7.8 billion annually. Google Cloud launched an AI startup innovation corridor from Southeast Asia to Silicon Valley, expanding collaborations with Singapore, Indonesia, and Vietnam. The Deputy Secretary-General of ASEAN received the Acting Director-General for Asia Affairs of Papua New Guinea's Department of Foreign Affairs to discuss enhancing relations. Thailand's exports grew for the 22nd consecutive month in April, largely due to front-loaded orders amidst supply-chain disruption concerns.

In South Korea, the military anticipates the wartime operational control transfer from the United States could occur by the end of 2026. A new 16,500 square meter robot theme park, Galaxy Robot Park, opened in Seoul, aiming to showcase robots in entertainment. The South Korean Ambassador to Nepal discussed strengthening bilateral relations, investment, and technology cooperation, noting that 120,000 Nepalis have benefited from the Employment Permit System.

### Africa
On Africa Day, May 25, 2026, the continent reflected on 63 years of the African Union, with calls for intensified Pan-African solidarity and concrete actions towards decolonization and sustainable development. Canada reaffirmed its commitment to deepening partnerships with Africa, focusing on trade, investment, peace, security, and climate resilience. In Senegal, political uncertainty deepened as Parliament Speaker El Malick Ndiaye resigned following President Bassirou Diomaye Faye's dismissal of Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko and the dissolution of the government.

A devastating gold mine landslide in Angola's Bengo province claimed at least 28 lives, highlighting the dangers of illegal mining. The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is grappling with its 17th Ebola Virus Disease outbreak in Ituri Province, with 513 suspected cases and 131 deaths reported as of May 19. Humanitarian efforts are complicated by insecurity, population displacement, and community mistrust. In Mali, escalating armed conflict has led to a humanitarian catastrophe, with 5.1 million people requiring assistance, and rising transportation costs and insecurity hindering aid delivery.

The African Development Bank, Africa50, and African leaders are exploring recycling public assets to invest in priority infrastructure, with Morocco named Africa's industrialization leader in a new index. South African police detained two Ethiopian nationals in connection with the alleged kidnapping of five fellow Ethiopians in Limpopo Province. A strong magnitude 5.0 earthquake occurred in the Indian Ocean south of Africa. Upcoming elections in 2026 are slated for 11 African countries, including Ethiopia and the Republic of the Congo, with a mix of competitive and predictable outcomes.

## SECTION 3 — Gulf & MENA Region
Diplomatic efforts are underway regarding a potential agreement between the U.S. and Iran to end the conflict, with reports of progress on many issues, including the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. However, Iran maintains that a deal is not imminent, citing U.S. position shifts and unresolved nuclear issues. Iran's President Masoud Pezeshkian has ordered the reopening of international internet access following a nearly 90-day blackout. Simultaneously, explosions were reported near Iran's Bandar Abbas city, with authorities stating the situation is under control.

In Israel and Palestine, tensions remain high. An Israeli airstrike in southern Gaza killed a Palestinian woman and a young girl in a displaced persons' camp, with 17 others wounded. This follows a ceasefire that has seen almost daily Israeli fire and a rising Palestinian death toll. Israeli opposition leader Yair Lapid stated that a two-state solution is not a priority for his bloc, emphasizing Israel's internal issues and tensions with Iran. He criticized Prime Minister Netanyahu for his handling of the Iran negotiations. Israeli occupation authorities also issued an order to seize a large tract of Palestinian-owned land near Jerusalem, under the pretext of "public interest" and "developing an archaeological site."

Economically, Saudi Arabia is leveraging the Iran conflict to bolster its position as a regional energy and logistics hub, with its East-West pipeline keeping oil exports flowing despite restrictions in the Strait of Hormuz. The IMF lowered Saudi Arabia's 2026 growth forecast but noted it was among the smallest downgrades in the Gulf region. The Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) marked its 45th anniversary, with non-oil sectors now accounting for 78% of its economy, indicating successful diversification. The UAE, Saudi Arabia, and other regional allies condemned Israeli National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir over the treatment of Gaza-bound flotilla detainees. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 4 — Technology and the Future
China launched its crewed Shenzhou-23 spacecraft with three astronauts, including the first Hong Kong astronaut, to its Tiangong space station. One astronaut will undertake a year-long mission, a record for China, as the country aims for a crewed moon landing by 2030. China has also initiated a national ID system for humanoid robots, assigning 29-character codes to over 28,000 robots across 200 models to track their lifecycle from production to recycling. In a significant scientific innovation, experimental samples co-developed by Binzhou Weiqiao UCAS Advanced Technology Research Institute were launched aboard the Tianzhou-10 cargo spacecraft to the Chinese Manned Space Station for in-orbit research on lightweight high-entropy alloys. This marks the first time private Chinese sci-tech forces have reached space.

In AI developments, Google I/O showcased AI-first search and announced Gemini 3.5 Flash, a more cost-effective and real-time AI model. Anthropic is reportedly nearing a $30 billion funding round, potentially pushing its valuation past OpenAI, driven by projections of significant quarterly revenue. Pope Leo XIV issued his first encyclical, "Magnifica Humanitas," calling for robust regulation of AI and emphasizing its development for the common good over profit, urging oversight from religious leaders, governments, and civil society. Fujitsu developed a self-evolving multi-AI agent technology that learns and adapts from daily operations, human feedback, and policy changes to continuously optimize business processes. The Canadian government is investing nearly $16.5 million in 13 businesses and organizations across the Greater Toronto Area to accelerate AI adoption and bring new AI technologies to market faster.

In robotics, Cornerstone Robotics, a Hong Kong-based innovator, received CE Mark approval for its Sentire® Endoscopic Surgical System, granting it access to European markets for minimally invasive surgical procedures. A robotics summit and expo is scheduled for May 27-28, 2026, in Boston, bringing together developers to discuss the latest technologies and trends. Berkeley hosted a community robotics showcase, connecting students with the tech industry and highlighting the importance of "deep-tech" in building real-world solutions. Sydney's life sciences sector reached a milestone with ION, a purpose-built commercial life sciences precinct, securing its first global partners to address the shortage of specialized infrastructure in Australia. A Qatar University virologist won the Vaccine Research and Innovation Award at the 8th Saudi International Vaccination Forum for contributions to vaccine development.

## SECTION 5 — Tech Industry & Global Business Shifts
The tech industry is grappling with significant shifts, largely driven by advancements in AI and ongoing cybersecurity threats. Meta has announced substantial layoffs, with approximately 8,000 employees affected, as the company reallocates resources towards AI investments. This move is part of a broader trend in the tech sector, where over 100,000 jobs have been cut in 2026 to fund AI initiatives, with companies like Amazon and Cisco also implementing reductions. WiseTech Global's CEO faced threats after public remarks about replacing human labor with AI, highlighting the contentious nature of AI-driven redundancies.

In terms of major tech announcements, Google I/O showcased AI-first search and introduced Gemini 3.5 Flash, a more efficient AI model. Anthropic is reportedly close to securing a $30 billion funding round, potentially elevating its valuation above OpenAI. Notably, Pope Leo XIV released his first encyclical, "Magnifica Humanitas," advocating for robust AI regulation and emphasizing its development for the common good, not just profit. The document critiques the "culture of power" in the AI race and warns against the technology's potential for domination and exclusion. Cybersecurity incidents continue to pose significant risks. The DragonForce ransomware group claimed a cyberattack on Spanish manufacturer Rolser, threatening data leaks. Radiology Associates of Richmond disclosed a data breach affecting 266,000 individuals, with names and protected health information compromised. North Korea-linked Lazarus Group deployed a cross-platform malware called RemotePE in attacks targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations.

In M&A and business developments, SpaceX, OpenAI, and Anthropic are all expected to go public, with their large valuations poised to influence major stock indexes. General Oceans acquired MRV Systems LLC to expand its capabilities in marine robotics and subsea technology. NLS Lighting acquired Schréder, gaining access to global resources and smart lighting technologies. Altron reportedly walked away from multiple M&A deals while surprising with a special dividend. FinVolution Group reported its Q1 2026 financial results, showing a decrease in net revenue but strong growth in international and programmatic channels. Sabio Holdings also announced Q1 2026 results, with core business revenues growing 6% year-over-year, excluding political and advocacy spending, and strong growth in international and programmatic channels.

## SECTION 6 — Science & Research Breakthroughs
In space physics, the NASA SPHEREx telescope captured a rare transitional phase of quasars, discovering 77 previously unknown objects hidden by dust clouds, providing strong evidence for this phase in galaxy evolution. The James Webb Space Telescope has yielded new findings, including observations of cloudy weather on an exoplanet and clues regarding the origins of one of Neptune's moons. China's Shenzhou-23 mission saw three astronauts launch to its space station, with one set for a year-long stay to study long-duration human physiology in space. Additionally, experimental samples for studying lightweight high-entropy alloys were launched to the Chinese Manned Space Station aboard the Tianzhou-10 cargo spacecraft.

In medical discoveries, the FDA granted approval for data-durstikin, the first TRP2-directed antibody-drug conjugate for first-line treatment of metastatic triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC), demonstrating a statistically significant overall survival advantage. The FDA also gave accelerated approval to heploex bulveride mod, the first and only approved treatment for chronic hepatitis delta virus (HTV). Scientists have debuted Reslins, an AI-powered genomic language model tool capable of detecting hidden antibiotic resistance genes previously missed by global databases. This marks a significant step in combating antimicrobial resistance. Qatar University virologist Dr. Hadi Mohamad Yassine received the Vaccine Research and Innovation Award for his contributions to vaccine development. UBC's Southern Medical Program is hosting MEDTalks to present scientific findings on diet, exercise, and blood sugar management.

In climate science, a study led by the Chinese Academy of Sciences revealed that powerful storms and drier air are accelerating biomass turnover in the Amazon rainforest, potentially reducing its long-term carbon storage capacity. Researchers predict a 3% to 15% shortening of carbon turnover time by the end of the century. New data also confirms that Antarctica's deep ocean has been heating up for the past 20 years, with warming seawater, melting glaciers, and polar ice sheets accelerating global sea level rise. Physicists with the ATLAS Collaboration at CERN's Large Hadron Collider (LHC) observed the Bc*+ meson, a new exotic particle, contributing to the understanding of the strong force. Sydney's life sciences sector saw a major milestone with the ION precinct securing its first global partners to address a shortage of specialized infrastructure.

## SECTION 7 — Global Health & Biosecurity Watch
The Democratic Republic of Congo (DRC) is facing its 17th Ebola Virus Disease outbreak in Ituri Province, which has resulted in at least 220 suspected deaths and over 900 suspected cases. The World Health Organization (WHO) has declared this outbreak a public health emergency of international concern, noting that the response is being outpaced by the rapidly spreading Bundibugyo strain, for which no approved vaccines or treatments exist. Neighboring Uganda has reported seven confirmed Ebola cases linked to the DRC outbreak, prompting enhanced screening at U.S. airports for travelers from DRC, Uganda, and South Sudan. Attacks on healthcare facilities and community mistrust are severely hampering containment efforts in the DRC.

In a significant global health development, the Seventy-ninth World Health Assembly adopted an updated Global Action Plan on Antimicrobial Resistance (GAP-AMR) for 2026–2036. This comprehensive "One Health" framework aims to accelerate progress against AMR through prevention-first interventions, responsible antimicrobial use, strengthened surveillance, and international cooperation. The WHO also established a joint process to support reforms of the global health architecture, acknowledging that the current framework has not kept pace with evolving global challenges like the rapid advancement of AI and digital technologies.

Regarding vaccine updates, the FDA is urging public comment on COVID-19 vaccine updates for Fall 2026, with a meeting scheduled for May 28, 2026, to discuss the antigen composition. The 2025-2026 COVID-19 vaccination guidance from the CDC recommends vaccination for individuals aged 6 months and older based on individual decision-making, with Moderna (Spikevax) being the only approved vaccine for children under 5. However, there are reports of federal vaccine policy changes, including the military no longer requiring flu vaccination and a reduction in recommended childhood immunization diseases by the CDC, sparking debate and criticism.

Biosecurity measures are being heightened globally. The UAE has tightened biosecurity at its ports with the arrival of over 664,000 livestock for Eid Al Adha, implementing stringent inspections and testing for zoonotic diseases. Türkiye is also conducting nationwide health and safety checks on over 4 million sacrificial animals. Highly pathogenic avian influenza (HPAI) continues to be a risk for poultry producers globally, with Pennsylvania recording 30 detections in early 2026. The World Organisation for Animal Health (WOAH) is launching the PREVENT Forum to boost animal vaccination programs worldwide.

## SECTION 8 — Global Security & Terrorism Monitor
Global security concerns are dominated by ongoing terrorism threats and cyber warfare. In the U.S., Customs and Border Protection (CBP) officers at the Hidalgo and Pharr International Bridges intercepted over $1.2 million worth of cocaine in two separate incidents, highlighting persistent drug smuggling challenges along the border. CBP also expanded health-screening programs at Atlanta and Houston airports for travelers arriving from Ebola-affected nations. The U.S. travel industry is concerned about potential threats to "sanctuary city" airports if Homeland Security withdraws CBP officers. The Department of War has no new advisories or headlines for May 25, 2026. The National Terrorism Advisory System (NTAS) also shows no current advisories, though previous alerts highlighted risks from false narratives, calls for violence against critical infrastructure, and foreign terrorist organizations.

Internationally, the Global Terrorism Index 2026 reported a substantial fall in global terrorism deaths and attacks in 2025, but a sharp increase in Western countries. Pakistan recorded the highest impact from terrorism, driven by a resurgence of activity from groups like Tehrik-e-Taliban Pakistan (TTP) and the Balochistan Liberation Army (BLA). The joint US-Israeli military operation against Iran is seen as greatly escalating the risk of future terrorism in the Middle East and beyond. Australia experienced one of its deadliest jihadist-inspired attacks on Bondi Beach in December, resulting in 15 deaths. The UK raised its national terrorism threat level to "SEVERE" following the Golders Green terrorist attack in April.

In cyber warfare, authorities in the Netherlands arrested co-owners of two internet hosting companies for operating IT infrastructure used by Russia to conduct cyberattacks and disinformation campaigns within the European Union. Taiwan's Administration for Cybersecurity plans to expand its cybersecurity attack and defense drills to include more critical infrastructure operators, reporting that China attempted to breach Taiwan's critical infrastructure an average of 2.63 million times last year. The North Korea-linked Lazarus Group deployed cross-platform malware named RemotePE in attacks targeting financial and cryptocurrency organizations. NIST published a draft guide focused on ransomware response and operational recovery in manufacturing networks, addressing mounting operational disruption risks. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 9 — Energy, Climate & Environment Watch
The global energy landscape is experiencing significant shifts, with crude oil markets reacting to geopolitical developments. Brent crude oil prices fell to $96.71/Bbl on May 25, 2026, a 6.60% drop from the previous day, primarily due to optimism surrounding potential U.S.-Iran peace talks and the anticipated reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. However, Iran's foreign ministry states that a deal is not imminent, and the U.S. blockade on Iranian ships will remain in effect until an agreement is signed, contributing to uncertainty and continued inflationary pressures. The International Energy Agency's (IEA) assumption of a late May reopening of Hormuz has not materialized, leading to concerns about inventory draws and potential shortages.

Renewable energy investments are surging worldwide, driven by rising demand for clean power and advancements in technology. BloombergNEF (BNEF) forecasts solar to become the world's largest electricity source by 2032, with a 17-fold surge in energy storage by 2035. The U.S. installed 9.7GWh of new battery energy storage systems (BESS) in Q1 2026. Canada-based Enbridge announced a $1.2 billion solar and battery energy storage project in Wyoming to power Meta's data centers, expanding their clean energy partnership. However, the U.S. Department of Agriculture has extended a pause on loans for controversial digesters that convert manure into biogas, citing high delinquency rates.

Extreme weather events continue to impact various regions. Europe is experiencing an unprecedented May heatwave, with France and the UK setting new national temperature records. Temperatures are expected to reach up to 40°C in parts of Spain by the end of the week. In California, the threat of an explosion from a damaged chemical tank in Garden Grove has been mitigated, but evacuation orders remain due to ongoing public health and environmental risks. Governor Gavin Newsom secured a Presidential Emergency Declaration for this hazmat incident. Severe weather alerts were issued for Davidson County, predicting heavy rainfall. In Australia, the Northern Tornadoes Project confirmed two tornadoes and a downburst in the London area on May 19, prompting warnings about AI-generated images causing confusion during genuine events. Massachusetts is addressing hazardous dams, with a significant number posing risks to communities downstream.

In climate policy, U.S. states and cities are developing parallel systems for climate diplomacy, with California, for instance, signing a memorandum of understanding with the UK government to support decarbonization efforts, despite federal policy opposition. The "Stop Climate Shakedowns Act of 2026" was introduced in the U.S. to shield oil companies from climate-related lawsuits. Europe's next long-term budget for 2028-2034 is being discussed, with concerns that the role of nature in the climate crisis is strategically underestimated. New data indicates Antarctica's deep ocean has been heating up for 20 years, contributing to accelerating sea level rise globally. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 10 — Global Economy & Market Signals
**Crude Oil:** Brent crude oil prices fell sharply by approximately 6-7% on May 25, 2026, trading near $90.30/Bbl for WTI and $96.71/Bbl for Brent. This decline was primarily driven by investor optimism regarding potential U.S.-Iran peace talks and the anticipated reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Despite the dip, oil prices remain significantly higher (around 50%) than a year ago due to the ongoing Middle East conflict and supply disruptions.

**Equities:** Global equity markets saw broad gains on May 25, with Asian indices leading the rally. Japan's Nikkei 225 surged to a record high above 65,000, and Taiwan stocks also reached all-time highs. U.S. stock index futures rose, though cash markets were closed for Memorial Day. European stocks also advanced, with the Stoxx 600 gaining for a sixth consecutive session. The positive sentiment is attributed to hopes for de-escalation in the Middle East and continued enthusiasm for AI-related investments.

**Currency:** The U.S. dollar weakened against most major currencies on May 25, with the DXY index falling by 0.25% to around 98.9909, as safe-haven flows moderated due to optimism about the Iran deal. The euro strengthened against the dollar, while the Japanese yen saw a slight depreciation. Trading activity was lighter due to holidays in the U.S. and UK.

**Central Banks:** Central bank decisions and outlooks remain a key focus. The U.S. Federal Reserve has kept its benchmark interest rate steady amid persistent inflation, with market expectations for a rate hike in 2026 remaining around 60%, despite the recent drop in oil prices. The European Central Bank (ECB) is considering a potential rate hike as early as June due to high inflation. The Bank of Israel is expected to cut rates, while the South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is anticipated to raise rates due to high oil prices and domestic inflation. The Reserve Bank of New Zealand (RBNZ) and Bank of Korea (BoK) will announce monetary policy decisions this week.

**Inflation indicators:** Inflation remains a significant concern globally. In the U.S., April's headline CPI inflation rose to 3.8%, and the upcoming core PCE inflation measure will be closely watched. Europe is also grappling with high inflation, particularly in energy costs, with provisional inflation figures for May from eurozone countries expected. In Australia, the April inflation rate is expected to ease slightly to 4.4%. Japan's capital will release inflation data, providing clues on price pressures amidst the Middle East conflict.

## SECTION 11 — Global Infrastructure & Mega Projects
Global infrastructure and mega-projects are seeing significant activity with a focus on smart city development, digital infrastructure, and large-scale construction. Turkmenistan unveiled its "Ashgabat-2045" smart city development strategy, focusing on modern infrastructure, digital public administration, clean energy networks, and sustainable urban services. BPX is leveraging digital twin systems to transform smart city development by providing real-time visibility and predictive analytics for urban planning and infrastructure optimization. Larimar City & Resort, a smart city project in the Dominican Republic, is positioning itself as a benchmark for international real estate investment.

In digital infrastructure, Kazakhstan is accelerating its Data Center Valley project to attract global AI companies and cloud computing leaders, with full-scale construction expected to begin in June 2026. This initiative aims to develop a domestic AI ecosystem and strengthen digital infrastructure. Biznet, an Indonesian digital infrastructure company, enhanced its connectivity by deploying Ciena's WaveLogic 5 Extreme on its submarine cable system, delivering 400G high-speed services and accelerating digitalization.

Major transport projects are underway globally. Canada's largest transit agencies are advocating for stronger federal support, facing over $50 billion in unfunded capital needs for major projects over the next decade. In the U.S., the House Transportation and Infrastructure Committee approved a $580 billion bill over five years for roads and bridges, with additional funds for railway safety. However, the bill also proposes cuts to transit and Amtrak funding. The Washington State Department of Transportation will restrict major construction during the FIFA World Cup to alleviate traffic. Halifax, Canada, is implementing a pilot project for adaptive traffic signals to improve traffic flow. Finland is developing a standard-gauge railway, "Rail Nordica," to enhance connections to the European rail network, focusing on cross-border logistics and security of supply.

Several mega-projects are nearing completion or undergoing significant shifts in 2026. NEOM in Saudi Arabia is recalibrating its focus from residential concepts to industrial utility. Noida International Airport in India is transforming into a regional transit anchor, and the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam is entering its final months of full operational output. The Sphere Abu Dhabi received a $1.7 billion contract and is set for completion by 2029, aiming to be a global destination for immersive entertainment. India's major infrastructure projects, totaling 1,981 projects, have recorded a cumulative cost overrun of ₹5.6 lakh crore as of April. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 12 — Migration & Demographic Trends
The Trump administration announced a significant shift in U.S. green card policy, now requiring most foreign nationals temporarily in the country to return to their home countries to apply for permanent residence. This change, effective immediately, limits in-country adjustment of status to "extraordinary circumstances" and could affect over 400,000 pending applications, potentially causing delays and increased costs for individuals and multinational companies. Canada, aiming to strengthen Indo-Pacific ties, is changing visa requirements for eligible travelers from Indonesia and Malaysia starting May 26, 2026, allowing them to apply for an electronic travel authorization (eTA) instead of a visa.

Global migration trends indicate that access to safe and regular migration pathways remains crucial for economic growth but is uneven. The World Migration Report 2026 highlights that restricting these pathways leads to more irregular and dangerous routes. As of mid-2024, an estimated 304 million international migrants were recorded, representing 3.7% of the world's population. Remittances continue to be a vital source of support, exceeding official development assistance and foreign direct investment combined.

The world population is estimated at 8,293,449,346 as of May 24, 2026, projected to reach 8.30 billion by July 1, 2026. The U.S. population is estimated at 349,035,494 at mid-year 2026. The U.S. Census Bureau provides detailed population estimates and projections, noting a net gain of one person every 36 seconds in the U.S.

Regarding refugee flows, the Trump administration has finalized a record-low refugee admissions ceiling of 7,500 for Fiscal Year 2026, primarily prioritizing Afrikaners from South Africa. This decision has drawn criticism from faith-based humanitarian organizations like Church World Service, which notes that over 120,000 rigorously vetted refugees remain stranded globally. The UN projects 136 million people will be forcibly displaced or stateless by the end of 2026. UNHCR reports that escalating hostilities in Iran and Lebanon have led to refugee returns to Afghanistan and Syria, highlighting the fragility of humanitarian situations in the Middle East. Shortfalls in global humanitarian aid and tighter mobility restrictions are intensifying pressures on displaced populations. This remains a developing story.

## SECTION 13 — India National (Politics & Governance)
President Droupadi Murmu conferred Padma awards in New Delhi on May 25, 2026, including the Padma Vibhushan posthumously to actor Dharmendra, received by Hema Malini, and to violinist N. Rajam. Padma Bhushan was awarded to former Maharashtra Governor Bhagat Singh Koshyari and banker Uday Kotak.

Fuel prices were raised by ₹2.61-2.71 per litre, marking the fourth increase in less than two weeks. Opposition leaders, including Congress president Mallikarjun Kharge and Rahul Gandhi, criticized the government, calling it "daily robbery" and Prime Minister Modi "inflation man".

The Supreme Court backed the Madhya Pradesh government's decision to transfer the former model-turned-actor Twisha Sharma's death case to the Central Bureau of Investigation (CBI). Intense heatwave conditions continued across India, with Delhi experiencing temperatures exceeding 43 degrees Celsius and its warmest May night in nearly 14 years. India is generally experiencing temperatures up to 47.6°C.

India's Commerce and Industry Minister Piyush Goyal stated that Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney's recent visit helped overhaul the Canada-India relationship, including a $2.6-billion uranium supply deal. Hundreds of Nagas protested in Imphal, Manipur, demanding the immediate release of six civilians allegedly held hostage by suspected Kuki militants. Sources indicate that India holds crude oil reserves for 78 days. Approximately 300 tourists were rescued after a technical issue with the Gulmarg Gondola caused panic.

## SECTION 14 — Telangana State Updates
Telangana Chief Minister A. Revanth Reddy has expressed concerns over the Central Government's failure to procure food grains at the Minimum Support Price (MSP), forcing the state to purchase farmers' produce itself. He also discussed providing houses for families affected by the Musi rejuvenation project and reforming the education system. Heatwave conditions have intensified across Telangana, with Dahegaon in Kumuram Bheem district recording the highest temperature at 46.5°C. Several districts in northern and eastern Telangana experienced temperatures above 45°C, with the state average at 42.9°C. At least 16 deaths due to severe heatwave conditions have been reported. Chief Minister Revanth Reddy reiterated that the state government is procuring maize despite the financial burden, criticizing the Union Government for not doing so. A family in Bhadrachalam was reportedly forced to carry a relative's body for nearly four kilometers after a government hospital allegedly failed to provide a hearse vehicle. As of May 25, 2026, only 3% of houselisting blocks for Census-2027 were completed in Telangana, and female enumerators will be accompanied by male counterparts when visiting farmhouses due to safety concerns.

---

## SECTION 15 — Andhra Pradesh State Updates
Andhra Pradesh Chief Minister N. Chandrababu Naidu announced the state's aim to promote one crore entrepreneurs under the 'one family-one entrepreneur' initiative, prioritizing the MSME sector. He virtually laid the foundation for 38 MSME parks and set a target of creating 100 unicorns in the state. The state government has released ₹558 crore in incentives for the MSME sector and plans to establish industrial parks in all constituencies. Chandrababu Naidu also highlighted plans to transform five lakh women from DWCRA groups into entrepreneurs this year, with women's self-help groups holding a corpus of ₹28,000 crore. A high-level ministerial sub-committee met to review excise reforms, revenue enhancement measures, and long-term policy decisions for the Prohibition and Excise Department. Discussions included strengthening beer supply, improving infrastructure, and addressing seasonal demand fluctuations. YSRCP supremo Y.S. Jagan Mohan Reddy alleged that democracy in Andhra Pradesh is under threat, accusing CM Naidu of using police machinery to suppress opposition voices and dissent.

---

## SECTION 16 — Complete MENA Strategic & Technology Developments
**Crude Oil:** Crude oil prices, both Brent and WTI, saw a significant downturn on May 25, 2026, falling by approximately 6-7%. This drop was largely influenced by growing optimism regarding a potential U.S.-Iran peace deal and the possible reopening of the Strait of Hormuz. Despite this daily decline, prices remain elevated, almost 50% higher than a year ago due to the ongoing Middle East conflict. The IEA's projection for inventory rebuilding, contingent on a late May Hormuz reopening, is now at risk as no agreement has been signed.

**Equities:** Global equity markets experienced a positive trend on May 25, with major indices closing higher. Asia-Pacific markets led the gains, with Japan's Nikkei 225 surging past 65,000 to a record high, and Taiwan stocks also reaching new peaks. U.S. stock futures showed strong gains, while European benchmarks also rose. This upward movement is attributed to hopes for de-escalation in the Middle East and sustained investor enthusiasm for AI and related technologies.

**Currency:** The U.S. dollar weakened against a basket of major currencies on May 25, with the DXY index declining by 0.25%. This was mainly due to a moderation in safe-haven flows as optimism grew around the potential U.S.-Iran deal. The euro and pound gained against the dollar. The yen, however, saw a slight depreciation.

**Central Banks:** Central banks globally are navigating a complex economic landscape marked by persistent inflation. The U.S. Federal Reserve has maintained its benchmark interest rate, with market pricing still anticipating a potential rate hike in 2026, despite the recent drop in oil prices. The ECB is signaling a possible rate hike in June, while the Bank of Israel is expected to cut rates due to economic contraction. The South African Reserve Bank (SARB) is projected to increase rates due to high oil prices and domestic inflation.

**Inflation indicators:** Global inflation remains a key concern. In the U.S., the April CPI rose to 3.8%, and the upcoming core PCE inflation data is closely watched. European countries, including Germany, France, and Italy, are also expected to release provisional inflation figures for May, reflecting the impact of high energy costs. Australia's April inflation rate is anticipated to ease to 4.4%. Japan's capital will also release its May core inflation rate.

---

## SECTION 11 — Global Infrastructure & Mega Projects
Global infrastructure and mega-projects are experiencing significant development and transformation, driven by smart city initiatives, digital infrastructure expansion, and large-scale construction efforts. Turkmenistan unveiled its "Ashgabat-2045" smart city development strategy, a 20-year master plan focusing on modern infrastructure, digital public administration, clean energy, and sustainable urban services. BPX is utilizing digital twin systems to enhance smart city planning, offering real-time visibility and predictive analytics for optimizing urban systems like transportation and energy. In the Dominican Republic, Larimar City & Resort is establishing itself as a benchmark for international real estate investment in smart city development.

In digital infrastructure, Kazakhstan is accelerating its "Data Center Valley" project in Ekibastuz to attract global AI companies and hyperscalers, with full-scale construction slated for June 2026. This initiative aims to foster a domestic AI ecosystem and bolster digital capabilities. Indonesia's Biznet has upgraded its submarine cable system with Ciena's WaveLogic 5 Extreme, providing 400G high-speed services to enhance inter-island and international connectivity and accelerate digitalization.

Transport infrastructure projects are progressing globally, albeit with varying challenges. Canada's largest transit agencies are seeking increased federal support, facing over $50 billion in unfunded capital needs for major projects over the next decade. In the U.S., a House committee approved a $580 billion bill for roads and bridges over five years, which also includes railway safety improvements but proposes cuts to transit and Amtrak funding. The Washington State Department of Transportation will limit major construction during the FIFA World Cup to ease traffic congestion. Halifax, Canada, is piloting adaptive traffic signals to improve traffic flow in busy corridors. Finland is advancing the "Rail Nordica" project, a standard-gauge railway, to improve cross-border logistics and military mobility within Europe.

Mega-projects are nearing completion or undergoing strategic adjustments. Saudi Arabia's NEOM project is pivoting towards industrial utility. India's Noida International Airport is becoming a critical regional transit hub, and the Grand Ethiopian Renaissance Dam is preparing for full operational output. Abu Dhabi's Sphere is under construction with a $1.7 billion contract, aiming for completion by 2029 as a major immersive entertainment venue. India's major infrastructure projects, encompassing 1,981 initiatives, have incurred a cumulative cost overrun of ₹5.6 lakh crore as of April. This remains a developing story.

---

## SECTION 12 — Migration & Demographic Trends
The Trump administration has enacted a significant shift in U.S. immigration policy, now requiring most foreign nationals currently in the country on temporary visas to return to their home countries to apply for green cards. This change, effective May 25, 2026, treats in-country adjustment of status as "extraordinary relief" and prioritizes consular processing abroad, a move that could impact over 400,000 pending applications and draw criticism from immigration advocates and business leaders. Exceptions are carved out for refugees, asylees, and certain dual-intent visa holders. The U.S. also implemented enhanced public health screening at designated airports (Washington-Dulles, Atlanta, and Houston) for travelers arriving from Ebola-affected nations (DRC, Uganda, South Sudan).

In global migration, the World Migration Report 2026 emphasizes that restricting safe and regular pathways pushes migrants towards more dangerous, irregular routes, despite migration's role in economic growth. By mid-2024, there were an estimated 304 million international migrants, representing 3.7% of the world's population, with remittances remaining a crucial financial support. Globally, the population is estimated at 8,293,449,346 as of May 24, 2026, projected to reach 8.30 billion by July 1, 2026. The U.S. population is estimated at 349.04 million for mid-2026.

Regarding refugee flows, the Trump administration has set a record-low refugee admissions ceiling of 7,500 for Fiscal Year 2026, with a primary focus on Afrikaners from South Africa. This decision has been met with strong condemnation from humanitarian organizations, which highlight that over 120,000 approved refugees remain stranded and the UN projects 136 million people will be forcibly displaced globally by the end of 2026. Escalating hostilities in Iran and Lebanon have exacerbated humanitarian consequences across the Middle East, leading to refugee returns to Afghanistan and Syria. Shortfalls in global humanitarian aid and tightening mobility restrictions are intensifying the pressures on displaced populations. This remains a developing story.

---

## SECTION 16 — Complete MENA Strategic & Technology Developments
The Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region is undergoing significant economic transformation and technological development, driven by diversification efforts, strategic investments, and structural reforms. Despite global economic moderation and geopolitical risks, the IMF projects MENA's GDP to grow in 2026 and achieve stable growth through 2030, exceeding the global average. This resilience is partly attributed to a resurgence in tourism, reduced political tensions, and increased industrial activity, alongside a commitment to economic diversification away from oil.

Smart city initiatives are at the forefront of urban innovation in the Arabian Gulf. Saudi Arabia's $500 billion NEOM project, particularly "The Line," is progressing with 40% of its first residential modules complete and AI neural network infrastructure being installed, with resident applications opening in Q2 2026. Dubai's "Smart City 2026" initiative is moving towards cognitive urban systems, with AI managing 40% of municipal services and implementing AI traffic management and predictive maintenance. Abu Dhabi is investing Dh42 billion (approximately $11.4 billion USD) in infrastructure, smart mobility, and sustainability projects, including launching Level 4 autonomous vehicles. Qatar's Msheireb Downtown Doha is leveraging its FIFA legacy to create a sustainable downtown with smart infrastructure for district cooling and water recycling. The GCC green buildings market is forecast to reach $95.33 billion by 2035, driven by sustainability regulations and smart city investments.

In technology, MENA startups are attracting significant investor interest across fintech, HR tech, proptech, and AI. Saudi Arabia's Arib raised $23.5 million for its digital financing marketplace, and Jordan's Manara Ventures launched a $70.5 million Shariah-compliant fund for tech startups. UAE proptech firm eVoost AI secured $2.2 million to expand its AI-driven property sales platform. RemotePass, a UAE-based workforce platform, raised $17.4 million to expand globally and invest in AI-driven compliance tools. Classera, in partnership with Zain KSA, launched a global initiative to integrate AI solutions into Saudi Arabia's educational ecosystem. The AI infrastructure boom is reshaping the Middle East's technology landscape, with accelerated growth of AI-ready data centers and a shift towards open infrastructure architectures, driven by demand for enterprise storage and sovereign AI initiatives. Egypt's digital healthcare IT market is also seeing modernization through universal health insurance, digital claims, and AI-enabled care operations. This remains a developing story.

---

## SECTION 17 — Social Media & Public Sentiment Trends
Public sentiment is increasingly influenced by geopolitical events and technological advancements. In the U.S., consumer sentiment has dropped to a record low, primarily driven by high petrol prices and lingering uncertainty around the U.S.-Iran conflict. Online debates are ongoing concerning the extent of free speech and content monetization, particularly following a shooting incident in Tennessee where a livestreamer charged with attempted murder had reportedly profited from hate-filled interactions. The #WalkAway Campaign is launching a YouTube program, "The Great MAGA Divide: Boomers vs Millennials," to explore generational political divides ahead of upcoming elections.

Protests and demonstrations were planned globally for May 25, 2026. In France, nationwide demonstrations were expected on May 26 over salary increases and bargaining negotiations across various public sectors. Members of the Congolese Diaspora planned a protest in Brussels, Belgium, against Rwandan officials for allegedly undermining the territorial integrity of the Democratic Republic of Congo. In Midrand, South Africa, protesters gathered at the Gallagher Convention Centre to advocate for employment policies prioritizing South African citizens. Chihuahua and Ciudad Juárez in Mexico were expecting demonstrations to pressure authorities to uphold democratic institutions and the rule of law. Nurses at Saint Mary of Nazareth Hospital in Chicago planned a protest against their employer for alleged illegal crackdown on unionization efforts.

Social media trends in May 2026 are characterized by the continued dominance of short-form video content, the rise of social media as a search engine, and an increasing interest in authentic, less polished posts. AI-assisted content planning is becoming commonplace, but overly machine-made content risks losing audience trust. Creator-led brand storytelling is gaining traction over traditional corporate messaging, and niche communities are proving more valuable than broad follower counts. UNESCO launched a "Build or Break" campaign in South Africa, encouraging young men to reject online misogyny and promote respect in digital spaces. Pope Leo XIV's encyclical "Magnifica Humanitas" reflects a global debate on AI's impact, warning against concentrated technological power and the risk of dehumanization. Digital rights activists emphasize that privacy-preserving internet infrastructure is crucial for human rights amidst rising censorship and shutdowns. This remains a developing story.

---

## SECTION 18 — Deep Analysis of Most Important Breaking News
**What Happened:** The Trump administration announced a new policy on May 25, 2026, requiring most foreign nationals who are temporarily in the U.S. and seeking green cards to return to their home countries and apply from abroad. This policy largely eliminates the "adjustment of status" process, which allowed individuals to apply for permanent residency from within the United States. Exceptions apply to certain categories like refugees, asylees, and H-1B visa holders.

**Root Causes:** This policy shift stems from the Trump administration's broader agenda to tighten immigration controls and reduce in-country filings for green cards. The stated intent is to ensure the immigration system functions as "the law intended," by preventing temporary visitors from using their stay as a direct pathway to permanent legal status. This move is also influenced by a desire to reduce the need for locating and removing individuals who might overstay their visas after being denied residency.

**Historical Background:** The ability for foreign nationals to adjust their status from within the U.S. has been a common pathway to lawful permanent residence for decades. This process, governed by Section 245 of the Immigration and Nationality Act, previously granted discretion to the Attorney General to adjust the status of eligible non-citizens. The new policy represents a significant departure from these established practices.

**Key Actors:** The primary actors are the Trump administration, specifically U.S. Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS) and the Department of Homeland Security (DHS), which issued the policy memorandum. Immigration advocacy groups like FWD.us and business leaders, particularly in the tech industry, are significant stakeholders who have voiced concerns about the policy's disruptive impact.

**Strategic Importance:** This policy holds strategic importance for several reasons. It signals a hardening stance on immigration, potentially setting a precedent for future restrictive measures. Economically, it could disrupt industries reliant on foreign workers, complicate talent management for multinational companies, and increase relocation costs. Socially, it could cause significant stress and uncertainty for hundreds of thousands of individuals and families. The policy also raises questions about America's competitiveness in sectors like AI, which depend on global talent.

**Possible Long-Term Consequences:** The long-term consequences could include a substantial increase in processing times for green card applications and a backlog at U.S. consulates abroad. It may also lead to a brain drain as highly skilled foreign workers find it more challenging to secure permanent residency in the U.S. The policy could further strain international relations and contribute to a perception of the U.S. as less welcoming to immigrants, potentially impacting the country's global standing and attractiveness for talent and investment.

---

## SECTION 19 — This Day in History (Global)
*   **1647:** Alse Young became the first person executed as a witch in the American colonies, in Hartford, Connecticut.
*   **1868:** The impeachment trial of U.S. President Andrew Johnson concluded with his acquittal by one vote in the Senate.
*   **1889:** The first Eiffel Tower elevator opened to the public.
*   **1896:** Charles Dow published the first edition of the Dow Jones Industrial Average.
*   **1908:** Engineers made the first major oil find in the Middle East at Masjed Soleyman in southwest Persia (modern-day Iran). The discovery significantly impacted the region's and world's economy and politics.
*   **1923:** The inaugural 24 Hours of Le Mans endurance race was held in France, with André Lagarde and Albert Leonard as the winners.
*   **1926:** Abd el-Krim surrendered to the French, ending the Rif War.
*   **1940:** Operation Dynamo began, a massive evacuation of Allied troops from Dunkirk, France, during World War II.
*   **1969:** The Apollo 10 astronauts returned to Earth after a successful eight-day dress rehearsal for the first crewed Moon landing.
*   **1972:** U.S. President Richard M. Nixon and Soviet leader Leonid Brezhnev signed the Anti-Ballistic Missile (ABM) Treaty in Moscow, regulating anti-ballistic missile shields during the Cold War.

---

## SECTION 20 — This Day in History (India)
*   **1739:** Mughal Emperor Muhammad Shah signed a treaty with Nadir Shah of Persia, leading to the separation of Afghanistan from the Indian empire.
*   **1885:** Govindagraj (Ram Ganesh Gadkari), a renowned Marathi playwright, comedy author, and poet, was born.
*   **1906:** Benjamin Peary Pal, an internationally acclaimed Indian agricultural scientist and plant breeder, was born. He later served as the first Director-General of the Indian Council of Agricultural Research.
*   **1926:** The notorious man-eating Leopard of Rudraprayag, which had claimed over 125 lives, was captured by famed hunter Jim Corbett, ending its reign of terror in the Garhwal region.
*   **1945:** Vilasrao Dagdojirao Deshmukh, a prominent Indian politician and former Chief Minister of Maharashtra, was born.
*   **1993:** The Centre decided to implement the Mandal Commission report, providing 27% reservation to backward castes, effective June 15, 1993.
*   **1997:** The Indian government decided to allow Liquefied Petroleum Gas (LPG) as motor vehicle fuel.
*   **1998:** S. Chandra Sekhar, a scientist, was awarded the Niels Bohr Unesco Gold Medal.
*   **1999:** The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) successfully launched three satellites, including the IRS P3, marking a significant achievement in India's space program. In sports, Sourav Ganguly and Rahul Dravid set a then-world record partnership of 318 runs for the second wicket in a One-Day International (ODI) match against Sri Lanka during the Cricket World Cup.
*   **2014:** Narendra Modi was sworn in as the 15th Prime Minister of India, representing a significant shift in the nation's political landscape.

---

## SECTION 21 — This Week in Review (Last 7 Days)
The past week (May 19-25, 2026) was dominated by diplomatic efforts surrounding the U.S.-Iran conflict. President Trump announced a postponement of military strikes on Iran, citing appeals from Gulf allies for more time for negotiations. Iran, while acknowledging some progress, expressed skepticism due to past U.S. actions and emphasized that a full deal is not imminent. This uncertainty kept global energy markets volatile, with oil prices remaining elevated due to concerns over supply disruptions in the Strait of Hormuz.

In the global economy, major stock markets showed resilience, with the S&P 500 reaching a new record high, largely driven by strong corporate earnings and continued bullish sentiment in the AI sector. However, underlying economic pressures persist, including hotter-than-expected inflation data and rising bond yields, leading to concerns about potential Federal Reserve rate hikes. Cisco Systems announced 4,000 job cuts to fund aggressive AI investments, reflecting a broader tech industry trend of AI-driven layoffs.

Human rights concerns were highlighted by a report revealing global executions reached the highest level since 1981 in 2025, largely driven by Iran. The World Health Organization declared an Ebola outbreak in the Democratic Republic of Congo a public health emergency of international concern, with rising cases in Uganda and attacks on health facilities hindering response efforts. The U.S. implemented enhanced health screenings at airports for travelers from affected regions.

In other notable events, the Israeli Navy intercepted a flotilla heading to the Gaza Strip, leading to international condemnation. In California, two teenage attackers fatally shot three people at the Islamic Center of San Diego, prompting a hate crime investigation. Debates continued around free speech and content monetization on social media platforms, particularly after a livestreamer charged with attempted murder had reportedly profited from hate-filled online interactions.

The French Open tennis tournament began in Paris, coinciding with an unprecedented May heatwave across Europe that saw record temperatures in France and the UK. Finland is planning a standard-gauge railway, "Rail Nordica," to enhance European connections.

---

## SECTION 22 — This Month in Review (From 1st of Current Month)
